narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 36 and a middle-income family background, your law school application stood out in some ways but faced challenges in others that the admissions model couldn't overlook. The model predicts there's a 55.6% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold that triggers an automatic rejection to protect the school's outcomes. This decision hinged mostly on your academic trajectory, particularly your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers—far below the typical student's midpoint around the 5th or 6th decile. That low Year 3 performance was the biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, as it signals to the model a potential struggle with the rigorous demands ahead, even if your Year 1 decile of 2 showed some early promise. Your LSAT score, while slightly below the average of 36.7, actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction toward success more than any other positive element. Meanwhile, your middle family income bracket nudged things slightly toward the riskier side compared to higher-income peers who often have more resources for exam prep. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.6, below the typical 3.21, and full-time student status—shared by over 90% of applicants—played supporting roles but weren't the main drivers. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having a competitive LSAT but weaker late-stage grades and GPA that dragged down the odds. To turn this around, focus on strengthening your academic record—perhaps through postgraduate courses, a master's program, or targeted tutoring to boost future decile rankings. Retaking the LSAT for a higher score could also shift the balance, given its strong positive influence here. Many applicants in similar situations reapply after addressing these gaps, so consider gathering letters of explanation or additional credentials to highlight your resilience. With deliberate steps like these, you can reshape how models—and admissions committees—view your potential.
